title: TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods
abstract: We propose TruthfulQA, a benchmark to measure whether a language model is truthful in generating answers to questions. The benchmark comprises 817 questions that span 38 categories, including health, law, finance and politics. We tested GPT-3, GPT-Neo/GPT-J, GPT-2 and a T5-based model. The best model was truthful on 58% of questions, while human performance was 94%. Larger models were generally less truthful, a result that is the inverse of the scaling found in other NLU tasks. The models generated many false answers that mimic popular misconceptions and have the potential to deceive humans.
domain: general (38 categories: health, law, finance, politics, etc.)
hallucination_rate: Best model 58% truthful (42% hallucination); larger models less truthful; human baseline 94% truthful

title: FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long Form Text Generation
abstract: We introduce FActScore (Factual precision in Atomicity Score), a new evaluation that breaks a generation into a series of atomic facts and computes the percentage of atomic facts supported by a reliable knowledge source. We evaluate people biographies generated by several LLMs. InstructGPT achieves a FActScore of 71.6, ChatGPT 71.5, and PerplexityAI 89.3. Retrieval augmentation and smaller more recent models show higher factual precision.
hallucination_rate: InstructGPT: 28.4% atomic facts unsupported; ChatGPT: 28.5% unsupported; PerplexityAI (retrieval-augmented): 10.7% unsupported

title: HaluEval: A Large-Scale Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are prone to generate hallucinations, i.e., content that conflicts with the source or cannot be verified by factual knowledge. To understand what types of content and to what extent LLMs are apt to hallucinate, we introduce HaluEval, a large-scale collection of generated and human-annotated hallucinated samples for evaluating the performance of LLMs in recognizing hallucinations. HaluEval includes 5,000 general user queries with ChatGPT responses and 30,000 task-specific examples from three tasks (QA, knowledge-grounded dialogue, text summarization). ChatGPT recognizes only about 62% of hallucinated content in its own output.
hallucination_rate: ChatGPT fails to recognize ~38% of hallucinations in its own output; hallucination recognition accuracy ~62%

title: HalluLens: LLM Hallucination Benchmark
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often generate responses that deviate from user input or training data, a phenomenon known as hallucination. This paper introduces a comprehensive hallucination benchmark, incorporating both new extrinsic and existing intrinsic evaluation tasks, built upon clear taxonomy of hallucination. We disentangle LLM hallucination from factuality, proposing a clear taxonomy that distinguishes between extrinsic and intrinsic hallucinations. Our benchmark includes dynamic test set generation to mitigate data leakage and ensure robustness.
hallucination_rate: Provides comprehensive benchmark rates across models; dynamic test sets to prevent data leakage saturation

title: A Survey on Hallucination in Large Language Models: Principles, Taxonomy, Challenges, and Open Questions
abstract: The emergence of large language models (LLMs) has marked a significant breakthrough in natural language processing (NLP), fueling a paradigm shift in information acquisition. Nevertheless, LLMs are prone to hallucination, generating plausible yet nonfactual content. This survey begins with an innovative taxonomy of hallucination in the era of LLM and then delves into the factors contributing to hallucinations. Subsequently, we present a thorough overview of hallucination detection methods and benchmarks. Our discussion then transfers to representative methodologies for mitigating LLM hallucinations.
models_tested: Survey covering GPT-3/4, ChatGPT, LLaMA, PaLM, and others
hallucination_rate: Survey paper aggregating rates from multiple studies; documents hallucination across QA, summarization, dialogue tasks

title: WikiChat: Stopping the Hallucination of Large Language Model Chatbots by Few-Shot Grounding on Wikipedia
abstract: This paper presents WikiChat, the first few-shot LLM-based chatbot that almost never hallucinates and has high conversationality and low latency. WikiChat is grounded on the English Wikipedia. Using a novel hybrid human-and-LLM evaluation methodology, we show that our best system achieves 97.3% factual accuracy in simulated conversations. It significantly outperforms all retrieval-based and LLM-based baselines. WikiChat achieves 97.9% factual accuracy in conversations with human users about recent topics, 55.0% better than GPT-4.
hallucination_rate: GPT-4 baseline: ~42.9% factual accuracy on recent topics; WikiChat: 97.9% factual accuracy (2.1% hallucination rate)

title: Hallucination in Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Survey, Taxonomy, and Mitigation Strategies
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance in a wide range of natural language processing operations, but their propensity to produce plausible-sounding, but factually incorrect information is often known as hallucination. We evaluate five top LLMs -- GPT-4, Claude 3 Opus, Gemini Pro 1.5, Llama 3 70B, and Mistral 8x7B -- on the TruthfulQA, HaluEval, and FActScore benchmarks and find hallucination rates of 18.7% to 34.2%. The RLHF+RAG hybrid achieves the best accuracy of 91.5%.
hallucination_rate: 18.7%--34.2% across five LLMs; RLHF+RAG hybrid reduces to 8.5%

title: How Much Do LLMs Hallucinate across Languages? On Realistic Multilingual Estimation of LLM Hallucination
abstract: We aim to quantify the extent of LLM hallucination across languages in knowledge-intensive long-form question answering (LFQA). We train a multilingual hallucination detection model and conduct a large-scale study across 30 languages and 6 open-source LLM families. Our analysis shows that LLMs hallucinate more tokens in high-resource languages due to longer responses, but that actual hallucination rates (normalized for length) seem uncorrelated with language digital footprint size. Smaller LLMs hallucinate more, and LLMs with broader language support display higher hallucination rates.
hallucination_rate: Varies by model and language; smaller models hallucinate significantly more; broader language support correlates with higher hallucination rates

title: Self-Logical Consistency Assessment of Large Language Models for Patient Feedback Classification
abstract: This study evaluated the Self-Logical Consistency Assessment (SLCA), a method designed to enhance LLM feedback classification reliability by enforcing a logically-structured chain of thought. SLCA reduced logical hallucinations among detected categories from 15.80% to 0.51% with GPT-4 and from 7.17% to 1.67% with Llama-3.1, with residual errors confined to the emergency feedback category.
hallucination_rate: GPT-4 baseline: 15.80% logical hallucination, reduced to 0.51% with SLCA; Llama-3.1: 7.17% reduced to 1.67%

title: Exploring the Boundaries of Reality: Investigating the Phenomenon of Artificial Intelligence Hallucination in Scientific Writing Through ChatGPT References
abstract: This study investigates the phenomenon of AI hallucination in scientific writing by examining references generated by ChatGPT. The study found that ChatGPT fabricated a significant proportion of scientific references when asked to generate citations, highlighting critical concerns about using LLMs for academic writing without verification.
hallucination_rate: High rate of fabricated references in ChatGPT-generated citations; landmark early study on citation hallucination

title: AI in conjunctivitis research: assessing ChatGPT and DeepSeek for etiology, intervention, and citation integrity via hallucination rate analysis
abstract: In this study, ChatGPT and DeepSeek are utilized as AI models to assist in diagnosis based on the responses generated to clinical questions. DeepSeek achieved a better performance with a 7% hallucination rate compared to ChatGPT's 13%. Claude demonstrated perfect 100% accuracy in binary classification, significantly outperforming ChatGPT's 62.5% accuracy.

title: Importing Phantoms: Measuring LLM Package Hallucination Vulnerabilities
abstract: We analyze package hallucination behaviour in LLMs across popular programming languages examining both existing package references and fictional dependencies. We discover that package hallucination rate is predicated not only on model choice, but also programming language, model size, and specificity of the coding task request. The Pareto optimality boundary between code generation performance and package hallucination is sparsely populated. We find an inverse correlation between package hallucination rate and the HumanEval coding benchmark.
hallucination_rate: Varies by model, language, and task specificity; inverse correlation with HumanEval scores; demonstrates security implications of code hallucination

title: Trustworthy AI for Medicine: Continuous Hallucination Detection and Elimination with CHECK
abstract: We present CHECK, a continuous-learning framework that integrates structured clinical databases with a classifier grounded in information theory to detect both factual and reasoning-based hallucinations. Evaluated on 1500 questions from 100 pivotal clinical trials, CHECK reduced LLama3.3-70B-Instruct hallucination rates from 31% to 0.3%. Its classifier generalized across medical benchmarks, achieving AUCs of 0.95-0.96, including on the MedQA (USMLE) benchmark.
hallucination_rate: LLama3.3-70B baseline: 31% hallucination; reduced to 0.3% with CHECK framework; GPT-4o USMLE passing rate boosted by 5pp to 92.1%

title: Measuring and Reducing LLM Hallucination without Gold-Standard Answers via Expertise-Weighting
abstract: LLM hallucination is currently a major threat to the trustworthiness and reliability of LLMs. We propose Factualness Evaluations via Weighting LLMs (FEWL), an innovative hallucination metric designed for scenarios when gold-standard answers are absent. FEWL leverages answers from off-the-shelf LLMs as a proxy of gold-standard answers. Extensive experiment results on TruthfulQA, CHALE, and HaluEval datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of FEWL.
models_tested: Multiple LLMs as reference models (evaluated on TruthfulQA, CHALE, HaluEval)
hallucination_rate: Provides calibrated hallucination measurement without gold answers; validated across TruthfulQA, CHALE, HaluEval

title: The Law of Knowledge Overshadowing: Towards Understanding, Predicting, and Preventing LLM Hallucination
abstract: We propose a novel concept: knowledge overshadowing, where model's dominant knowledge can obscure less prominent knowledge during text generation. Central to our approach is the log-linear law, which predicts that the rate of factual hallucination increases linearly with the logarithmic scale of (1) Knowledge Popularity, (2) Knowledge Length, and (3) Model Size. Built on overshadowing effect, we propose CoDa decoding strategy, which notably enhances model factuality on Overshadow (27.9%), MemoTrap (13.1%) and NQ-Swap (18.3%).
hallucination_rate: Hallucination rate follows log-linear law with knowledge popularity, length, and model size; CoDa reduces hallucination by 13.1-27.9% on benchmarks
